name: Data Types, Formats, and Storage
description: Understanding structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data and how each is stored and processed.
key_facts: Structured data is organized in tables with defined schemas, like relational database records
Semi-structured data has flexible schemas using formats like JSON, XML, or CSV
Unstructured data has no predefined format; examples include images, videos, and documents
OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) optimizes for frequent reads/writes of individual records
OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) optimizes for complex queries over large historical datasets
common_misconceptions: CSV files are not truly structured data; they are semi-structured because schema is not enforced
OLTP and OLAP are workload types, not specific technologies; many databases support both

name: Data Roles: Engineer, Analyst, and Scientist
description: The distinct professional roles in a data team and the responsibilities each carries within an organization.
key_facts: Data Engineers build and maintain data pipelines and infrastructure for collecting and storing data
Data Analysts query data and create reports and visualizations to support business decisions
Data Scientists apply machine learning and statistical methods to extract predictive insights
Database Administrators (DBAs) manage the performance, security, and availability of databases
Business Intelligence (BI) developers create dashboards and reports from data warehouses
common_misconceptions: Data scientists and data analysts are not the same; scientists build predictive models, analysts interpret historical data
Data engineers and DBAs overlap but differ; engineers focus on pipelines, DBAs on database operations

name: Relational Database Concepts
description: Foundational concepts of relational databases including tables, keys, normalization, and SQL.
key_facts: Relational databases organize data in tables (relations) with rows and columns
Primary keys uniquely identify each row; foreign keys establish relationships between tables
Normalization reduces data redundancy by organizing data into related tables
Indexes speed up data retrieval at the cost of slower writes and additional storage
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for querying and manipulating relational data
common_misconceptions: Normalization is not always desirable; denormalized schemas improve read performance in analytics
Indexes do not always speed up queries; full table scans can be faster for small tables

name: Azure SQL Services
description: Microsoft's family of managed relational database services on Azure for various SQL Server scenarios.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database is a fully managed PaaS relational database with automatic patching and backups
Azure SQL Managed Instance is near 100% compatible with SQL Server for lift-and-shift migrations
SQL Server on Azure VMs (IaaS) provides full SQL Server control but requires OS and patch management
Azure Database for PostgreSQL and MySQL are managed open-source relational database alternatives
Elastic pools in Azure SQL Database allow multiple databases to share resources and reduce costs
common_misconceptions: SQL Managed Instance and SQL Database are different; MI supports more legacy SQL Server features
Azure Database for MySQL is not the same as MySQL on a VM; it is fully managed by Microsoft

name: Non-Relational Data and NoSQL Concepts
description: Data stores that do not use the tabular model of relational databases, optimized for flexible schemas and scale.
key_facts: Document stores persist data as JSON documents with flexible schemas (e.g., MongoDB, Cosmos DB SQL API)
Key-value stores use a simple hash map structure with fast lookups (e.g., Redis, Cosmos DB Table API)
Column-family stores organize data in column families for high write throughput (e.g., Cassandra)
Graph databases store nodes and edges for modeling and querying relationships (e.g., Cosmos DB Gremlin API)
NoSQL databases typically sacrifice ACID compliance for horizontal scalability and flexibility
common_misconceptions: NoSQL does not mean 'no SQL'; many NoSQL databases support SQL-like query languages
NoSQL is not always faster than SQL; performance depends on the access patterns and use case

name: Azure Cosmos DB
description: Microsoft's globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database service designed for high availability and low latency.
key_facts: Cosmos DB supports multiple APIs: SQL (Core), MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin (Graph), and Table
Global distribution allows Cosmos DB to replicate data across multiple Azure regions automatically
Cosmos DB guarantees single-digit millisecond read/write latencies at the 99th percentile
Consistency levels in Cosmos DB range from Strong to Eventual, trading consistency for performance
Request Units (RUs) are the currency of throughput; all database operations consume a calculated number of RUs
common_misconceptions: Choosing an API in Cosmos DB does not change the underlying storage engine; it changes the query interface
Strong consistency in Cosmos DB increases latency and reduces availability; it is not always the right choice

name: Azure Blob Storage for Data
description: Object storage for unstructured data including data lake scenarios, backups, and big data analytics.
key_facts: Blob Storage stores unstructured data in containers (logical groupings) within storage accounts
Block blobs are optimal for large binary files; Append blobs are optimized for logging; Page blobs for random access
Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 adds hierarchical namespaces to Blob Storage for analytics workloads
Access tiers (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive) optimize cost for different data access frequencies
Blob Storage integrates with Azure Synapse Analytics and Azure Databricks for big data processing
common_misconceptions: Blob Storage and Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 are not separate services; ADLS Gen2 is Blob Storage with a feature enabled
Archive tier data is not immediately accessible; rehydration to Hot or Cool tier is required

name: Analytics Workloads and Data Warehousing
description: Large-scale analytical processing patterns and services for business intelligence and reporting.
key_facts: Data warehouses store historical, structured data optimized for analytical queries using columnar storage
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) moves data from source systems into the warehouse after transformation
ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) loads raw data first and transforms in the warehouse using compute
Azure Synapse Analytics is an integrated analytics service combining data warehouse and big data
Dimension tables store descriptive attributes; fact tables store measurements in a star schema
common_misconceptions: A data lake is not a data warehouse; lakes store raw data in any format, warehouses store structured processed data
ETL and ELT are not interchangeable; ETL transforms before loading, ELT leverages warehouse compute for transformation

name: Azure Synapse Analytics
description: An integrated analytics service that brings together data integration, enterprise data warehousing, and big data analytics.
key_facts: Azure Synapse combines SQL pools (data warehouse), Spark pools (big data), and Pipelines (orchestration)
Dedicated SQL Pool provides distributed data warehouse compute with massively parallel processing (MPP)
Serverless SQL Pool allows on-demand querying of data in the data lake without provisioning
Apache Spark in Synapse supports Python, Scala, R, and .NET for big data processing
Synapse Studio is the unified web-based IDE for all Synapse development and management tasks
common_misconceptions: Serverless SQL Pool in Synapse does not store data; it queries data in Azure Data Lake on demand
Synapse Analytics is not just a data warehouse; it is a full analytics platform with data integration

name: Azure Data Factory and Data Integration
description: Managed cloud ETL/ELT service for orchestrating and automating data movement and transformation at scale.
key_facts: Azure Data Factory (ADF) is a cloud-based ETL and data integration service for building data pipelines
Pipelines are logical groupings of activities that perform a unit of work
Linked Services define connection information to data sources and compute environments
Datasets represent data structures within the linked data stores
Integration Runtime provides the compute infrastructure for data movement and transformation
common_misconceptions: ADF is not for real-time streaming; it is designed for batch data movement and transformation
ADF does not store data; it moves and transforms data between source and destination stores

name: Real-Time Analytics and Stream Processing
description: Processing and analyzing data as it arrives, enabling immediate insights from continuous event streams.
key_facts: Stream processing analyzes data in motion as it flows through a system, enabling real-time insights
Azure Event Hubs is a big data streaming platform that ingests millions of events per second
Azure Stream Analytics is a serverless real-time analytics service for processing streaming data
Windowing functions in Stream Analytics group events by time (tumbling, hopping, sliding windows)
Azure Synapse Real-Time Analytics (formerly KQL DB) uses Kusto Query Language for time-series data
common_misconceptions: Event Hubs and Service Bus serve different purposes; Event Hubs is for high-throughput streaming, Service Bus for reliable messaging
Real-time does not always mean sub-millisecond; near-real-time processing with seconds latency is common

name: Microsoft Power BI for Data Visualization
description: Microsoft's business intelligence platform for creating interactive reports, dashboards, and data visualizations.
key_facts: Power BI Desktop is the Windows application for authoring reports and data models
Power BI Service is the cloud platform for publishing, sharing, and collaborating on reports
Power BI Mobile enables consuming reports on smartphones and tablets
Datasets in Power BI contain the data model including tables, relationships, and measures
DirectQuery mode queries the data source in real time; Import mode caches data in Power BI
common_misconceptions: Power BI Desktop and Power BI Service are not the same; Desktop is for authoring, Service for sharing
DirectQuery is not always better than Import; Import mode is faster for end users but may have stale data

name: SQL Query Fundamentals
description: Core SQL statements and clauses for querying, inserting, updating, and deleting data in relational databases.
key_facts: SELECT retrieves data; WHERE filters rows; JOIN combines tables; GROUP BY aggregates rows
DDL (Data Definition Language): CREATE, ALTER, DROP for defining schema objects
DML (Data Manipulation Language): INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE for manipulating data
Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX operate on groups of rows
Indexes use B-tree or hash structures to speed up specific query patterns
common_misconceptions: INNER JOIN returns only matching rows; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table even without a match
ORDER BY without TOP/LIMIT does not guarantee row retrieval order in some database engines

name: Data Ingestion and Processing Pipelines
description: Patterns and services for moving data from source systems to storage and analytics platforms.
key_facts: Batch processing collects data over a period and processes it as a group; lower latency requirements
Stream processing processes data continuously as it arrives; requires near-real-time response
Lambda architecture combines batch and stream processing for comprehensive analytics
Data pipelines automate the flow from ingestion through transformation to consumption
Data quality validation should occur at ingestion to prevent garbage data from corrupting downstream systems
common_misconceptions: Batch and stream processing are not mutually exclusive; many architectures use both
Lambda architecture adds operational complexity; Kappa architecture (stream-only) is an alternative

name: Transactional Data Systems and ACID Properties
description: Properties that guarantee reliable database transactions and how they apply to Azure relational services.
key_facts: ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state to valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions independent), Durability (committed data persists)
Transactions group multiple operations so they either all succeed or all fail together
Relational databases enforce ACID by default; many NoSQL databases relax these for scalability
Azure SQL Database supports full ACID transactions and read-committed snapshot isolation by default
CAP Theorem: a distributed system can guarantee only two of Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
common_misconceptions: Cosmos DB offers tunable consistency, not full ACID at the global level by default
ACID compliance does not prevent application-level data errors; it only ensures transaction integrity
